task1.6: The chart shows an increase in sales from 2010 to 2015. In 2010 sales were around 20 units, and they rose to about 45 units by 2015. The biggest rise happened between 2012 and 2013.
task1.7: Overall, sales grew steadily over the period shown. Starting at roughly 20,000 units in 2010, figures climbed to approximately 52,000 by 2015, with a particularly sharp increase in the middle years before levelling off slightly at the end.
task1.8: Overall, there was a marked upward trend in sales throughout the period. Having stood at just under 20,000 units in 2010, sales rose progressively, peaking at around 55,000 in 2014, after which they plateaued, finishing marginally lower at about 52,000 in 2015.
task1.9: The data depict a sustained expansion in sales between 2010 and 2015, punctuated by a mid-period surge. From an initial position of just shy of 20,000 units, volumes increased substantially—most notably between 2012 and 2014—before stabilising, with 2015 recording a figure only marginally below the preceding peak.

task2.6: Some people think technology is good for children but others think it is bad. I think both ideas are partly true. Parents should control screen time.
task2.7: While some argue that technology benefits children’s education, others contend that it can be harmful if used excessively. In my view, a balanced approach—with clear limits and guidance—is essential to maximise advantages and minimise risks.
task2.8: The debate over children’s use of technology hinges on whether digital tools chiefly enhance learning or undermine well-being. I would argue that, provided usage is purposeful and supervised, the educational gains can outweigh the drawbacks, though blanket restrictions may sometimes be warranted.
task2.9: Whether technology serves as a boon or a bane for younger learners depends less on the medium itself than on how it is integrated into daily routines. Properly curated, it can democratise access to knowledge; left unchecked, it risks displacing foundational skills—hence the imperative for nuanced, evidence-informed household policies.

letter.6: Dear Sir, I am write to complain about the noise. It is very bad and I cant sleep. Please fix it. Yours, Ali
letter.7: Dear Sir or Madam, I am writing to express my dissatisfaction with the noise from the construction site near my flat. The disturbance continues late into the night, which affects my sleep. I would appreciate it if you could investigate and advise on possible mitigation measures. Yours faithfully, Ali Valiyev
letter.8: Dear Mr Thompson, I am writing with regard to the recurrent noise generated by renovation works in the adjacent building. Despite previous assurances, disruptions persist well beyond permitted hours, considerably affecting my ability to work from home. I would be grateful if you could liaise with the contractor and clarify the agreed schedule. Yours sincerely, Ali Valiyev
letter.9: Dear Ms Rahman, I trust you are well. I am compelled to raise concerns about ongoing noise nuisance stemming from refurbishment activities at 14 Brook Lane, which routinely extends past the council’s stipulated cutoff. As a resident directly impacted, I should be grateful for a timeline confirming compliance measures and, where feasible, advance notice of exceptionally noisy phases. Yours sincerely, A. Valiyev
